title: Pentagon Puts $12.6B on Affordable Cruise Missiles, Anduril Drone Fighter Fires Live AIM-120, US Troops Quietly Leave Estonia
subhead: The Pentagon's affordable-mass missile program reaches $12.6B in framework agreements, Anduril's autonomous CCA fires its first live air-to-air missile, and the quiet drawdown of US forces from the Baltics raises NATO burden-sharing questions — even as Lockheed bags $13B in contracts and the Navy's hypersonic program falls two years behind.

sections.heading: Pentagon Bets $12.6B on 28,000 Affordable Cruise Missiles
sections.body:
The US Air Force's Family of Affordable Mass Missiles (FAMM) program reached a milestone on July 16, signing framework agreements with Anduril (Barracuda-500), CoAspire (Rapidly Adaptable Affordable Cruise Missile), and Zone 5 Technologies (Rusty Dagger) for up to 28,000 missiles over five years at $12.6 billion, according to Air Force Times and Defense Department announcements.

The core proposition: replace expensive precision munitions with cheaper alternatives. A Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile (JASSM) costs over $1.3 million per round; FAMM targets a unit cost near $218,000. The program splits into a lugged variant for fighters and bombers (FAMM-L) and a palletized variant dropped from airlifters (FAMM-P), both with ranges of 250–500 miles.

Anduril said its seven-year framework begins deliveries in 2027. The Air Force intends to buy up to 8,000 FAMM rounds per year across all vendors. The deals are framework agreements — not missile orders — setting terms and giving companies reason to invest in production capacity. Actual purchases depend on congressional approval of multiyear procurement.

Notably, Zone 5 Technologies, which runs its US operation from California, was acquired in June by Norwegian defense company Kongsberg, bringing a foreign defense prime into the US affordable-weapons supply chain. The same three companies, plus Leidos, were tapped in May for the Army-led Low-Cost Containerized Missiles program — a parallel ground-launched effort.

Undersecretary for Acquisition Michael P. Duffey said FAMM showed the department 'expanding the defense industrial base, fielding capabilities faster, and attracting private investment' — language straight from Defense Secretary Hegseth's Acquisition Transformation Strategy.
sections.whyItMatters: FAMM represents a genuine shift in Pentagon procurement philosophy: quantity has a quality all its own, and the US is betting that cheap missiles by the thousand can saturate enemy air defenses more effectively than a handful of $1M+ weapons. If the framework converts to real orders, it reshapes the standoff munitions market and opens it to nontraditional entrants.

sections.heading: Anduril's YFQ-44A Autonomous Fighter Fires First Live Missile
sections.body:
Anduril Industries' YFQ-44A Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA) fired its first live AIM-120 air-to-air missile during a test at Edwards Air Force Base on July 15, the company and Air Force Chief of Staff Gen. Ken Wilsbach confirmed.

Mark Shushnar, Anduril's VP of Autonomous Airpower, said the test was 'more than a simple weapons release' — it demonstrated an 'end-to-end, beyond-line-of-sight strike against a simulated target,' with the aircraft's Lattice software latching onto a target track and an operator tasking the aircraft to engage.

The milestone comes just weeks after the Air Force awarded CCA production contracts to both Anduril and General Atomics. Anduril has been producing the 'Fury' drones at its Arsenal 1 plant in Pickaway County, Ohio, since March. Shushnar said the FQ-44 has 'the ferry range necessary to deploy anywhere in the world' and 'a combat radius that significantly exceeds the combat radius for current crewed fighters.'

Separately, Anduril also secured a slot in the FAMM framework (see above), making it the week's most visible defense newcomer — now with stakes in both autonomous air combat and affordable mass strike.
sections.whyItMatters: A pilotless combat aircraft firing a live AIM-120 is a threshold moment for autonomous warfare. The CCA program is no longer theoretical — it's in live-fire testing. If Anduril's Fury reaches operational deployment, it would give the Air Force a low-cost, attritable wingman for manned fighters, fundamentally changing the economics of air-to-air combat.

sections.heading: US Quietly Pulls Hundreds of Troops from Estonia, Canceling Poland Rotation
sections.body:
US military personnel in Estonia have fallen to fewer than 100, down from several hundred deployed last winter, EconoTimes reported, citing Estonian Defence Minister Hanno Pevkur. While US military leaders indicate another rotation could arrive later this summer, the deployment remains under review.

The drawdown follows the Trump administration's decision to cancel a planned rotation of about 5,000 US troops to Poland, a move that also affected deployments in Lithuania and other Baltic states. The shift comes despite NATO's Ankara summit emphasizing stronger European defense commitments.

Many of the newly announced NATO projects — including the multinational missile program and the Canadian-led defense bank — will take years to become operational. Meanwhile, frontline allies like Estonia and Poland face immediate concerns about hybrid warfare threats. AI-based analysis from Omniforecaster estimated an 18% chance of a deadly clash between Russia and NATO members before end of 2026.

The US has also reduced personnel at several Middle East bases, repositioning assets to Jordan, Israel, Turkey, and mainland Europe — reflecting Washington's increasing focus on countering China while pressing European allies to take greater responsibility for regional defense.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between NATO summit announcements and on-the-ground force posture is widening. Summits produce spending pledges and consortium agreements, but the actual US military footprint in the Baltics is shrinking. For frontline states, the signal is unmistakable — and it comes at a moment when Russia's hybrid warfare campaign shows no signs of abating.

sections.heading: Lockheed Martin Wins $13B in Contracts Including $10.5B SOCOM Deal
sections.body:
Lockheed Martin secured three major Pentagon contracts on July 15 totaling roughly $13 billion, according to Defense Department contract announcements.

The largest is a $10.53 billion, 12-year global logistics and sustainment contract from US Special Operations Command (USSOCOM) — the largest special operations support contract ever awarded. Lockheed will provide operational and logistics support for SOF missions worldwide through August 2038.

A second contract covers $1.6 billion in F-35 spare parts for the Air Force, Marine Corps, Navy, and foreign customers over seven years, funded from 2026 aircraft procurement budgets. The third is a $439 million ATACMS guided missile and launching assembly sale to Taiwan, with completion expected by February 2031.

The SOCOM contract underscores how heavily the Pentagon still relies on Lockheed for sustainment of existing platforms even as it pushes toward cheaper, nontraditional alternatives like FAMM. The ATACMS sale to Taiwan also signals continued US commitment to arming Taipei with standoff strike capability despite Chinese objections.
sections.whyItMatters: Lockheed's $13B day is a reminder that legacy prime contractors aren't going anywhere — even as the Pentagon opens doors to Anduril and other newcomers. The SOCOM deal alone is a decade of revenue security. But the juxtaposition is stark: the same week the Pentagon bets on $218K missiles from startups, it also writes a $439M check for legacy ATACMS rounds.

sections.heading: Navy's Hypersonic Missile Program Two Years Behind, Costs Soar
sections.body:
The US Navy's effort to install Conventional Prompt Strike (CPS) hypersonic missiles on three Zumwalt-class destroyers is two years behind schedule, according to a Government Accountability Office report. Missile flight testing originally planned for 2025 has been pushed to 2027.

The program's cost has ballooned from a 2020 estimate of $31 billion for 262 missiles to $41 billion for 224 missiles — fewer weapons for $10 billion more. Lockheed Martin, the CPS missile body prime contractor, 'faces significant production issues,' GAO said, including problems with heat-resistant coatings, substandard parts, and insufficient production capacity. The factory can currently produce only six to seven missiles per year against a desired rate of 12.

The report also flagged a lack of coordination between the Navy and Army, which shares the CPS program through its Land-Range Hypersonic Weapon (LRHW) variant. GAO found that while the Navy is responsible for missile body production, the Army hired its own consultant to study production increases, creating potential for 'additional delay and inefficient use of taxpayer funds.'

The Zumwalt destroyers themselves suffer from unreliable power systems and difficulty obtaining spare parts — problems that predate the hypersonic installation. One ship needed 'more cabling than was anticipated' for CPS launch tube installation, adding further delays.
sections.whyItMatters: At a moment when the Pentagon is betting on cheap, mass-produced missiles for the future, its flagship hypersonic program is a case study in how the traditional acquisition system fails. $41 billion for 224 missiles that won't fly until at least 2027 — with production at half the needed rate — is the polar opposite of FAMM's philosophy.

sections.heading: Ukraine Hits 159 Russian Shadow Fleet Vessels in 12-Day Drone Campaign
sections.body:
Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces struck 159 vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet during a 12-day drone campaign codenamed 'Molochka,' according to commander Robert Brovdi. On July 17 alone, Ukrainian sea drones hit 12 vessels — nine dry cargo ships, one oil tanker, one gas carrier, and one tugboat — in the Black Sea.

The operation, running since July 6, has targeted vessels in both the Sea of Azov (117 struck) and the Black Sea (42 struck). Kyiv says the campaign aims to disrupt the transportation of oil and other goods used to evade international sanctions.

Separately, Ukraine's SBU reported striking two shadow fleet tankers in the Black Sea on July 16, while the Omega special forces unit said it hit a Russian Su-24M frontline bomber at Saky air base in occupied Crimea with a drone strike.

The scale of the maritime drone campaign is unprecedented. Ukraine has effectively demonstrated that a non-naval state can impose significant costs on commercial shipping through unmanned surface vessels — a template that other nations, particularly Gulf states seeking to counter Iranian drone threats, are studying closely.
sections.whyItMatters: 159 vessels struck in 12 days is not harassment — it's a sustained anti-shipping campaign conducted entirely by drones. This is a new form of naval warfare with implications far beyond the Black Sea: any state with maritime drone technology can now threaten commercial shipping at scale, and Ukraine is already exporting that expertise to Gulf partners.

sections.heading: Pentagon Awards $86M for 500-kW Joint Laser Weapon System
sections.body:
The Department of Defense awarded initial contracts worth $86 million — with a total potential ceiling of $847 million — to Lockheed Martin Aculight and nLIGHT Defense to develop the Joint Laser Weapon System (JLWS) capable of outputting up to 500 kilowatts, New Atlas reported.

The program, managed under the Scaled Directed Energy (SCADE) Critical Technology Area by the Office of the Under Secretary of War for Research and Engineering, will first deploy a 150-kW prototype to meet urgent operational needs, then scale to intermediate outputs of 300–500 kW, culminating in a fully integrated 500-kW system.

A 500-kW laser would represent a significant leap over current deployed systems (typically 50–150 kW), giving the military the ability to neutralize not just individual drones but 'high-velocity anti-ship weapons, next-generation cruise missiles, and dense saturation swarms,' according to the report. However, a system of this magnitude would require a 40-foot shipping container to house the weapon, cooling system, and power storage — a significant mobility constraint.

Under Secretary Emil Michael said the department is 'partnering with industry to rapidly deliver deep magazine directed energy capabilities to the Joint Force that can be seamlessly deployed across multiple domains.'
sections.whyItMatters: If the 500-kW laser reaches the field, it closes a key gap in counter-drone and anti-missile defense: current lower-power lasers struggle against fast, armored targets. But the engineering challenges — power storage, thermal management, beam coherence over distance — remain formidable, and the program's timeline is uncertain.

bottomLine: The Pentagon is placing two bets at once: a $12.6B gamble on cheap, mass-produced cruise missiles from startups like Anduril, and the continued flow of billions to Lockheed Martin for legacy sustainment and hypersonic programs that are years behind and billions over budget. Meanwhile, Anduril's CCA just fired its first live missile — a milestone for autonomous air combat — and the US is quietly drawing down troops in the Baltics even as NATO allies worry about the gap between summit pledges and on-the-ground reality. Ukraine's shadow fleet campaign, hitting 159 vessels in 12 days with drones, continues to demonstrate what unmanned warfare looks like at scale.
sources: Air Force Times, Defense News, USNI News, New Atlas, EconoTimes, Kyiv Independent, RFE/RL, War on the Rocks, The Diplomat, AL.com, GAO, Yahoo Finance, United24 Media, Anduril Industries, US Department of Defense contract announcements
